segments.paradiso-16-g23: Per tanti rivi s’empie d’allegrezza la mente mia: 'Per' introduces the means or cause ('by/through so many streams'); 'tanti rivi' is a plural noun phrase ('so many streams') placed before the verb for poetic emphasis. 'S’empie' is the pronominal present/passive-style form of 'empiere' (si + empie) meaning 'is filled' or 'fills itself' (reflexive clitic s’ + verb), and the affected noun 'la mente mia' follows, with the possessive 'mia' after the noun (poetic word order) meaning 'my mind.'
segments.paradiso-16-g24: che di sé fa letizia perché può sostener che non si spezza: 'che' introduces a relative clause modifying 'la mente' (or the previous idea). 'Di sé' ('of itself') emphasizes that the mind itself 'fa letizia'—literally 'makes joy' (fa + noun = causes or becomes joy). 'Perché' is causal 'because'; 'può' is the present indicative of 'potere' ('can/is able to'); 'sostener' (infinitive) here means 'to endure/withstand' (an active infinitive following 'può'); the final subordinate 'che non si spezza' uses 'si' as reflexive/passive and 'spezza' (present) meaning 'it does not break.'
segments.paradiso-16-g25: Ditemi dunque,: 'Ditemi' is the imperative form of 'dire' with the enclitic pronoun 'mi' ('tell me'); the verb is an addressed command/request. 'Dunque' is an adverb meaning 'then' or 'therefore', linking this request to what precedes.
segments.paradiso-16-g26: cara mia primizia,: a vocative phrase addressing the addressee. 'Cara' (adjective) + possessive 'mia' + noun 'primizia' (literally 'first-fruits' used metaphorically as a term of endearment). The order (adjective before possessive) is poetic but functions as 'my dear first-fruits.'
segments.paradiso-16-g27: quai fuor li vostri antichi e quai fuor li anni che si segnaro in vostra püerizia;: This is an archaic/poetic phrasing asking 'which were your ancients (forebears) and which were the years that were marked in your childhood.' 'Quai' and 'fuor' are archaic forms (equivalent to modern 'quali' and 'furono'); 'li vostri antichi' = 'your ancestors'; 'si segnaro' is a reflexive/personal passive past (segnarono / were marked) with 'si' impersonalizing the verb; 'in vostra püerizia' = 'in your childhood/childlikeness.'
segments.paradiso-16-g28: ditemi de l’ovil di San Giovanni quanto era allora,: another imperative 'ditemi' ('tell me') followed by 'de l’ovil di San Giovanni' ('about the sheepfold of Saint John') where 'de' = 'di' = 'about/of'. 'Quanto era allora' asks 'how much/how large it was then' — 'quanto' as interrogative/degree word and 'era' the imperfect indicative 'was,' locating the time.
segments.paradiso-16-g29: e chi eran le genti tra esso degne di più alti scanni».: 'E chi eran' = 'and who were' (archaic 'eran' = 'erano'). 'Le genti' = 'the people', 'tra esso' = 'among it/it being the sheepfold' (esso refers to the ovil), and 'degne di più alti scanni' = 'worthy of the higher seats' — 'degne' (worthy) + 'di' + plural noun 'scanni' (seats).
segments.paradiso-16-g30: A simile introduced by Come. s’avviva is the pronominal form (si + avviva) used impersonally/passively in the 3rd singular present: 'is enlivened / revived'. a lo spirar d’i venti is a prepositional phrase giving the cause or means ('by the blowing/breath') — note the poetic contractions lo (the) and d’ for di.
segments.paradiso-16-g31: così ties the simile to the main clause. vid' io is the contracted passato remoto vidi ('I saw'); the apostrophe marks elision. quella luce risplendere uses the infinitive risplendere as the complement of vid' (I saw that light shine). a’ miei blandimenti = ai miei blandimenti (to/in response to my blandishments).
segments.paradiso-16-g32: This is a continuation of the simile: e come introduces another parallel. a li occhi miei = to my eyes; si fé is si + fé (archaic past of fare, here reflexive) yielding the sense 'became / made itself' — 'it made itself more beautiful' = 'it seemed/was made more beautiful'.
segments.paradiso-16-g33: così again links the two parallels. con voce is an instrumental phrase ('with a voice'); più modifies the adjectives dolce e soave ('sweeter and more gentle'), both describing the voice.
segments.paradiso-16-g34: Negation and contrast: ma non con questa moderna favella denies that the speech used was the contemporary (vernacular) manner of speaking; favella is an older word for 'speech/tongue'.
segments.paradiso-16-g35: dissemi is a contracted, archaic spelling of disse + mi ('he/it said to me'); it introduces direct speech. The form is 3rd-person past plus enclitic mi.
segments.paradiso-16-g36: Temporal clause introduced by Da quel dì che (From that day when...). fu detto is the passato remoto passive of dire ('was said/was uttered'). al parto = 'at the childbirth'; in che mia madre begins a relative clause ('in which my mother...') that continues in the next lines.
segments.paradiso-16-g37: Ch’ = che (that/who) elided. è is present of essere ('is'); or is the archaic adverb for 'now', and santa means 'holy'/'saint'. The short clause states the mother is now a saint.
segments.paradiso-16-g38: s’allevïò is si + alleviò (passato remoto of alleviare) meaning 'she was relieved' (pronominal use); di me marks what she was relieved of ('of me'); ond’ (onde) = 'from which/whence' and era grave gives the reason ('which had been a burden'). Note the diaeresis in allevïò marks the vowel break in pronunciation.
segments.paradiso-16-g39: The clause has a somewhat inverted, poetic order. al suo Leon names the astrological locus ('at her Leo', i.e. when Leo was in the sky). The numerals cinquecento cinquanta e trenta enumerate the number of times (500 + 50 + 30 = 580). fiate (fiate) here means 'times' (occasions); venne ... a rinfiammarsi = 'this fire came to rekindle' — venne + a + infinitive expresses that the fire 'came to' / 'was caused to' rekindle. sotto la sua pianta = 'under her root/plant' (pianta = base/plant/sole in poetic usage).
segments.paradiso-16-g40: The opening phrase Li antichi miei e io places the ancestors before 'I' but the verb nacqui is 1st-person singular passato remoto: the verb agrees with 'io' (common in medieval usage to keep the verb in the 1st person). nel loco dove si truova is an impersonal/passive present (si truova = 'is found') introducing a relative locative clause. pria l’ultimo sesto da quei che corre il vostro annüal gioco indicates position 'before the last sixth belonging to/those of whom your annual course runs' — da quei che corre marks the agent/sequence of the annual circuit.
segments.paradiso-16-g93: Grand' is the poetic contraction of grande (apostrophe because the following vowel begins the next word). Era is the imperfect/past-tense 'was' (3rd singular of essere) and già means 'already' — together 'was already'. La colonna (lit. 'the column') here means a political or social column/formation; del Vaio is the prepositional phrase 'of the Vaio' (del = di + il) naming the group or place.
segments.paradiso-16-g94: A single proper name listed as part of the column; the comma signals a simple apposition in a list of families or people.
segments.paradiso-16-g95: Another item in the list; capitalized Giuochi appears as a name here rather than the common noun 'games', so it functions as a proper noun in apposition.
segments.paradiso-16-g96: A continuation of the list of family names (Fifanti, Barucci, Galli) joined by the conjunction e 'and'. Quei 'those' begins a relative clause (ch' = che) meaning 'those who…'; arrossan is an archaic 3rd‑person plural present indicative (they redden/blush). Per + lo staio indicates the cause or object of the blushing: 'for/on account of the staio' (staio = a measure/bushel).
segments.paradiso-16-g97: Lo ceppo 'the stock/lineage' — ceppo here means family stock/lineage. Di che nacquero is a relative clause 'from which they were born/sprung' (nacquero = 3rd plural past of nascere). I Calfucci names the family; era già grande is again 'was already large/great' describing the lineage's importance.
segments.paradiso-16-g98: E già eran tratti is 'and already had been drawn/selected' — eran is the imperfect 'they were', tratti is a past participle used here in a passive sense ('had been chosen / appointed'). A le curule: 'to the curule chairs' (curule = magistral/curule chairs). Sizii e Arrigucci are the specific curule offices or family names associated with those offices. Note: tratti here is a learned/political sense ('chosen/drawn for office') rather than the common senses of trattare.
segments.paradiso-16-g99: Oh/ O is the exclamatory vocative interjection. Quali io vidi 'what sort(s) I saw' — quali is 'what kind' (introducing an exclamation). Quei che son disfatti is a resumed demonstrative + relative clause: 'those who are undone/ruined' (son = are, disfatti = past participle meaning undone/destroyed). Per lor superbia means 'because of/their pride' (per = through/for; lor = their); superbia is the abstract noun 'pride' (Dante's usage), here causing their ruin.
segments.paradiso-16-g100: The clause begins with the coordinating e 'and'. Le palle de l'oro 'the balls/spheres of gold' (le palle = spheres/rounded emblems); de l' is the contracted of + the. Fiorian is an archaic 3rd‑person plural verb form meaning 'they caused to bloom' or 'they made flourish' (Dante uses -an for 3rd plural). Fiorenza is the direct object here (Florence). In tutt' i suoi gran fatti 'in all her great deeds' — tutt' i = all the, suoi = her/its, gran = great, fatti = deeds/undertakings.
segments.paradiso-16-g1: O is an interjection/vocative particle addressing an abstract quality. 'poca' is an adjective (feminine) modifying 'nobiltà'; the possessive 'nostra' precedes the noun in the normal Italian order. 'di sangue' is a genitive/qualifying prepositional phrase meaning 'of blood' (i.e. by lineage). The whole line is a direct address to an abstract attribute.
segments.paradiso-16-g2: 'se' begins a conditional clause 'if'; the sequence 'glorïar di te la gente fai' is causative: 'fai' (you make) + infinitive 'glorïar' — you cause people to glorify/boast of you. Word order puts the infinitive before the subject 'la gente' for poetic inversion. 'qua giù' is a locative adverbial 'here below'; 'dove' introduces a relative clause describing that place; 'l’affetto nostro langue' has 'affetto' as subject and 'langue' as verb (languish), with 'nostro' agreeing with the noun.
segments.paradiso-16-g3: 'mirabil cosa' is an adjectival phrase (adjective before noun in poetic register). 'non mi sarà mai' uses the future indicative ('sarà') with dative clitic 'mi' meaning 'to me'; the negative 'non...mai' = 'never'. The structure means 'it will never be a marvel to me' (i.e. I will not be astonished by it).
segments.paradiso-16-g4: 'ché' = 'for/because' introduces the explanatory clause. 'là dove' = 'there where'; 'appetito' is the subject ('desire'); 'non si torce' is a reflexive/impersonal present ('is not turned/twisted') — 'si' marks a passive/reflexive sense and 'torce' is 3rd person singular. The clause means 'because there, where desire is not perverted...'.
segments.paradiso-16-g5: 'dico' is 1st person present 'I say' and introduces a parenthetical remark. 'nel cielo' is a prepositional phrase 'in heaven' qualifying where the following claim holds or is being said emphatically.
segments.paradiso-16-g6: Personal emphatic pronoun 'io' starts the clause; 'me ne gloriai' is a perfective/simple past form ('gloriai' = I boasted/took pride), with clitic 'ne' = 'of it/about it' and reflexive 'me' for the verb 'gloriarsi' (to take pride). The whole means 'I took pride in it.'
segments.paradiso-16-g7: 'Ben se’ tu' is an emphatic/poetic form = 'Well/indeed are you' (se’ is an archaic/separated form of 'sei'). 'manto' is a metaphorical noun 'cloak/mantle' and the relative clause 'che tosto raccorce' uses 'tosto' (soon/quickly) with 'raccorce' (3rd singular present of 'raccorciarsi'/'raccorcare' = 'shorten/contract'). The clause describes something that quickly becomes shorter.
segments.paradiso-16-g8: 'sì che' is a conjunction meaning 'so that' or 'therefore', introducing a consequence or result clause. 'che' functions as the subordinating particle.
segments.paradiso-16-g9: This conditional clause 'se non s’appon di dì in die' is archaic in form. 'se' = 'if'; 's’appon' is reflexive/impersonal 'si appongono' (are/are placed, literally 'add themselves'); 'di dì in die' is an idiomatic expression meaning 'day by day' (literally 'of day into day'). The sense: 'if they are not added/accumulate day by day.'
segments.paradiso-16-g10: Subject 'lo tempo' (time) + verb 'va' (goes) with adverb 'dintorno' = 'goes around' or 'circles about'. 'con le force' uses an archaic form 'force' (= forze/forces/strengths); the phrase means 'time moves/circles with the forces' (poetic/abstract expression).
segments.paradiso-16-g11: 'Dal' = da + il 'from the' introducing the source ('Dal “voi”' — the use of the pronoun 'voi'). The relative clause 'che prima a Roma s’offerie' is archaic: 'prima' = formerly/previously; 's’offerie' is an older reflexive/conditional/past sense of 'si offrire' meaning 'was/used to be offered' or 'presented itself' in Rome. The phrase points back to an earlier usage.
segments.paradiso-16-g12: 'in che' = 'in which'; 'la sua famiglia' is the noun phrase 'his/that family/household/lineage'; 'men' is an archaic adverb meaning 'less'; 'persevra' is present indicative 'perseveres/continues'. The clause says that in that usage his family is less persistent/less involved.
segments.paradiso-16-g13: Compound past verb 'ricominciaron' (3rd pl. past) placed before the subject for poetic inversion: 'ricominciaron le parole mie' = 'my words resumed/began again.'
segments.paradiso-16-g14: 'onde' here functions as a discourse connective meaning 'whereupon'/'therefore'/'hence' (not the literal 'wave' meaning). It introduces the reaction of Beatrice; the following proper name is the subject.
segments.paradiso-16-g15: Relative clause 'ch’era un poco scevra' = 'who was a little scevra' where 'scevra' (feminine to agree with Beatrice) means 'free from' or 'a little unreserved/at ease'. 'un poco' moderates the adjective.
segments.paradiso-16-g16: Present participle/gerund 'ridendo' = 'laughing', used adverbially to describe Beatrice's manner as the main clause follows.
segments.paradiso-16-g17: 'parve' is the remote past of 'parere' (3rd sing.) meaning 'seemed'; 'quella che tossio' is a demonstrative + relative clause 'that one who coughed'. The prepositional phrase 'al primo fallo scritto di Ginevra' modifies the coughing: 'at the first mistake written about Ginevra' (Ginevra here is a woman's name). The syntax links the simile: she seemed like someone who coughed at the first fault.
segments.paradiso-16-g18: 'Io' is the emphatic subject pronoun; 'cominciai' is the 1st person singular past (I began) introducing direct speech. The colon marks the reported speech that follows.
segments.paradiso-16-g19: Direct address in the speech: 'Voi siete' is present indicative 2nd person plural/formal 'you are'; 'il padre mio' is a noun phrase 'my father' used vocatively. The pronoun 'voi' here is the respectful/plural form.
segments.paradiso-16-g20: 'voi mi date a parlar' uses 'date' (you give) + infinitive 'parlar' with a prepositional 'a' to express purpose/result: 'you give me to speak' = 'you give me the courage/permission to speak'. 'tutta baldezza' is an object phrase 'all/complete boldness' (tutta modifying a feminine noun in poetic usage).
segments.paradiso-16-g21: 'voi mi levate' is present 'you raise me' or 'you exalt me'; 'sì' is an adverb 'so'/'so much' strengthening the following result clause. The comma before the result clause is typical of Dante's syntactic rhythm.
segments.paradiso-16-g22: Result clause introduced by 'ch’' = 'che/that/so that'. 'i’ son' is an archaic inversion of 'io son' = 'I am'; 'più ch’io' is a comparative 'more than I' — together the paradoxical meaning is 'you raise me so that I am more than myself.'
segments.paradiso-16-g41: Basti is a third-person form (subjunctive/optative) of bastare used idiomatically as an injunction: “let it suffice” or “let this be enough.” D’i is the elided di + i = “of the,” and miei maggiori is a noun phrase “my elders/ancestors” (maggiori is plural and agrees with miei). Udirne is the infinitive udire + enclitic ne (‘of them’/‘about them’) meaning “to hear of them,” and questo is the demonstrative object “this” referring to the brief account just mentioned.
segments.paradiso-16-g42: This is a relative/indirect clause giving what one should only hear: chi ‘who’, ei an archaic nominative pronoun meaning ‘they’ (referring to the elders), si is the clitic appearing with the verb in Dante’s word-order, and fosser is the imperfect subjunctive (archaic form of fossero) of essere: “who they were.” E onde introduces the second coordinate clause “and whence,” and venner is the past historic (vennero) “they came”; quivi is the adverb “there / to that place.”
segments.paradiso-16-g43: Più è here begins a comparative clause: più (rather/more) + è (is) with the infinitive tacer (“to be silent”) functioning as the predicate. Che works as a comparative conjunction here = “than,” and ragionare onesto is an infinitive phrase literally “to speak/reason honourably” (onesto qualifying the manner). The meaning: it is more proper to be silent than to speak about them.
segments.paradiso-16-g44: Tutti color ‘all those’ is the subject; ch’ introduces a relative clause ‘who.’ A quel tempo = “at that time” (a = at), eran is the imperfect ‘were,’ and ivi = ‘there.’ Da poter arme is an idiomatic medieval/poetic phrase: da + infinitive (poter) with arme ‘arms/weapons’ gives the sense ‘fit/able to bear arms’ or ‘capable of taking up arms.’ Tra Marte e ’l Batista names the limits/area (“between Mars and the Baptist”), with the article contraction ’l = il before Batista.
segments.paradiso-16-g45: Eran il quinto = ‘they were the fifth’ (i.e. one-fifth). Di quei = ‘of those,’ and ch’or son vivi is a relative clause: ch’ (che) = who, or = now, son = are (present), vivi = living. The clause contrasts the past (eran) with the present (son vivi): those men constituted one-fifth of the people who are alive now.
segments.paradiso-16-g46: Ma = but; la cittadinanza is the feminine noun phrase ‘the citizenry’ or ‘the citizenship’ serving as subject. The comma shows a relative clause follows describing it.
segments.paradiso-16-g47: ch’è or mista = che (which) + è (is) + or (now) + mista (mixed): a short relative clause meaning “which is now mixed.” Mista is feminine to agree with cittadinanza.
segments.paradiso-16-g48: di Certaldo e di Fegghine lists the places whose people have mixed into the citizenry; di marks belonging/origin with the proper names Certaldo and (the obscure) Fegghine, coordinated by e.
segments.paradiso-16-g49: Pura vediesi ne l’ultimo artista is an impersonal/passive expression: pura (feminine) = ‘pure’; vediesi = si + vedere in inverted order meaning ‘is seen’ or ‘appears’; ne = ‘in’ or ‘among’; l’ultimo artista = ‘the last artisan/master.’ Literally: ‘the citizenry appears pure in the last artisan.’ Note agreement: pura refers back to la cittadinanza.
segments.paradiso-16-g50: Oh quanto fora meglio esser vicine = exclamative plus conditional. Oh quanto = “Oh how much”; fora is an archaic conditional form (here meaning “would be”) used with esser (infinitive) to express a wished-for result: “how much better it would be to be close.” Vicine agrees in gender/number with quelle genti (feminine plural). Note: fora is an archaic/poetic form (conditional) rather than the modern verb ‘forare’.
segments.paradiso-16-g51: e al Galluzzo e a Trespiano aver vostro confine continues the wish: e = and; al / a + place names = ‘at/to Galluzzo and to Trespiano’; aver vostro confine = infinitive ‘to have your boundary’ (aver = avere), i.e. to set the border there. The infinitive phrase is coordinated with esser vicine as desired outcomes.
segments.paradiso-16-g52: che averle dentro e sostener lo puzzo introduces the contrast with che = than. Averle = avere + clitic le (‘to have them’ — le referring back to quelle genti). Dentro = inside; e sostener = and to endure (sostenere) with lo puzzo = the stench. Grammatically the two infinitives averle and sostener are parallel objects of the comparative construction.
segments.paradiso-16-g53: di quel da Signa = a short appositive: di quel = ‘of that one’ (repeating/referring to the villan), da Signa = ‘from Signa’ (da indicating origin). The phrase specifies which villan is meant (the one from Signa).
segments.paradiso-16-g54: che già per barattare ha l’occhio aguzzo is a relative clause modifying the villan: che = who/that; già = already/indeed (intensifier); per barattare = ‘for bargaining’ or ‘when it comes to haggling’ (per + infinitive); ha l’occhio aguzzo = idiom ‘has a sharp (keen) eye’ (occhio aguzzo) meaning he is quick/astute in bargaining. The whole clause accuses him of shrewdness in trade.
segments.paradiso-16-g55: This is the conditional protasis introduced by Se (if). Se begins the contrary-to-fact clause and is followed by a relative clause (la gente ch’ al mondo più traligna) describing which people. Non fosse stata is a past subjunctive (pluperfect subjunctive in form) used here in an unreal conditional ('had not been'). The phrase a Cesare noverca uses a personal dative/a-phrase 'to Caesar' with the noun noverca 'stepmother.'
segments.paradiso-16-g56: Contrasts with ma (but) and gives a simile introduced by come 'like/as': come madre a suo figlio benigna is a short clause where madre is the noun and a suo figlio marks the beneficiary with a; benigna is an adjective agreeing with madre and describes the favorable stance that is being hypothesized.
segments.paradiso-16-g57: Tal fatto è fiorentino states the main point: tal fatto ('such a deed') + present indicative è. The coordination e cambia e merca links two verbs (or verbal senses) that describe what happens to that deed — it 'changes' (passes from hand to hand) and 'is traded/marketed' — both present-tense general statements.
segments.paradiso-16-g58: The relative/result clause introduced by che links back to 'tal fatto' and gives the hypothetical result: si sarebbe vòlto is a conditional perfect/conditional of direction ('would have turned') with the reflexive si; a Simifonti is a destination phrase naming the family/place that would have received it.
segments.paradiso-16-g59: Là dove introduces a locative relative clause 'there where…' and andava is the imperfect tense 'used to go' describing habitual action in the past; l’avolo is an archaic form for 'the ancestor' (the article contracted l’ + avolo), and a la cerca uses a + definite article + noun to indicate purpose/place (here 'to the search/hunt').
segments.paradiso-16-g60: This clause continues the conditional series with sariesi (an archaic conditional form meaning 'would be' or a dialectal/metrical variant) followed by the place-name Montemurlo and ancor 'still/yet'; de’ Conti (di + Conti) identifies possession/association — 'would have been still of the Conti.'
segments.paradiso-16-g61: Sarieno is a regular conditional form 'would be' agreeing with the plural subject i Cerchi; nel piovier d’Acone is a locative/genitive phrase naming the fief/family (in the Piovier of Acone). The sentence continues listing hypothetical transfers of property/family names.
segments.paradiso-16-g62: E forse introduces a final possibility ('and perhaps'); in Valdigrieve is another locative phrase (in the Val di Greve) and i Buondelmonti names the family that might have held that place. The whole list completes the apodosis of the initial conditional.
segments.paradiso-16-g63: Sempre 'always' sets a general statement about cause: la confusion de le persone is a noun phrase ('the confusion of persons') and principio fu uses principio as predicate nominative with fu (past simple of essere) meaning 'was the origin' of the subsequent genitive phrase del mal de la cittade ('of the city's evil').
segments.paradiso-16-g64: Come introduces a comparison 'as/just as'; del vostro il cibo marks possession ('your food') and the relative clause che s’appone uses the clitic s’ (si) with appone to form a passive/impersonal sense 'that is placed/supplied' — s’appone here is an impersonal or reflexive present meaning 'is put on/laid in.'
segments.paradiso-16-g65: This is a short proverbial comparison: e introduces it; cieco toro (blind bull) is subject, più avaccio cade uses più + avaccio (an adverb meaning 'sooner/more quickly') with the present indicative cade 'falls', and che introduces the standard of comparison 'than' followed by cieco agnello (blind lamb).
segments.paradiso-16-g66: Another comparative proverb: e molte volte ('and many times') introduces a generalization; taglia is third-person singular 'cuts' (used figuratively); più e meglio (more and better) qualify una 'one' (one man/one action) and the clause che le cinque spade uses che as 'than' comparing the single person's effect to 'the five swords' (i.e. many weapons).
segments.paradiso-16-g67: Se tu riguardi is a conditional 'if you look' with the 2nd-person present riguardi; Luni e Orbisaglia are direct objects (place-names) and come sono ite uses come + present-of-being + past participle ite to mean 'as they have gone/been gone' — an observation about past decline.
segments.paradiso-16-g68: E come se ne vanno uses se (reflexive si) + ne (particle 'away/from there') with vanno 'they go' to mean 'and how they go away from it'; di retro ad esse is a locative phrase 'behind them/following them' and names Chiusi e Sinigaglia as following places.
segments.paradiso-16-g69: Udir 'to hear' heads an infinitive clause 'to hear how…' (used as subject/accusative construction here): come le schiatte si disfanno reports the content 'how the lineages (le schiatte) disband themselves' (si disfanno reflexive/plural present); non ti parrà uses the future parrà 'will seem' with negation non — 'it will not seem to you' — followed by nova cosa né forte 'a new thing nor a remarkable one.'
segments.paradiso-16-g70: Poscia che is a causal conjunction 'since/after that', introducing the reason clause; le cittadi termine hanno uses termine 'limit/end' as the predicate with hanno 'have' to state that cities have limits — supporting why such news is not surprising.
segments.paradiso-16-g71: Le vostre cose tutte is a topicalized noun phrase 'all your things'; hanno lor morte uses hanno (present) with lor morte ('their death') as a possessed noun phrase — a general statement that everything you own has its end.
segments.paradiso-16-g72: Sì come voi is a short comparative 'just as you [do]'; sì come functions like 'just as/so too' and voi is the addressed plural pronoun (you).
segments.paradiso-16-g73: Ma introduces contrast. Celasi is a reflexive verb form (si + celare) meaning 'it hides itself' or 'it is hidden'; in alcuna ('in some [cases/places]') marks where it hides; che dura molto is a relative clause modifying alcuna: dura is present tense 'lasts' and molto 'long/very.'
segments.paradiso-16-g74: Concluding clause: e le vite son corte uses son (present Copula) for 'are' with vite 'lives' as subject and corte 'short' as predicate adjective — a pithy generalization closing the thought.
segments.paradiso-16-g101: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' and opens the statement. Facieno is a medieval/poetic 3rd-person plural verb (equivalent to modern facevano / 'did' or 'used to do') and here introduces the action; the subject i padri ('the fathers' / forefathers) follows the verb by inversion, and di coloro begins the genitive 'of those' which is completed by the relative che in the next clause.
segments.paradiso-16-g102: Sempre che functions as a subordinating conjunction 'whenever / whenever it happens that'. La vostra chiesa is a straightforward possessive noun phrase 'your church'. Vaca is the archaic/poetic form of vacare (3rd singular) meaning 'is vacant' (the papal/church office is empty) and here belongs to the subordinate temporal clause.
segments.paradiso-16-g103: Si is the reflexive/impersonal pronoun 'themselves' (they make themselves). Fanno is 3rd-person plural present (or present-habitual in narrative) of fare and with grassi gives the result 'they grow/get fat' (become prosperous). Stando is the gerund of stare 'by staying / while sitting', and a consistoro specifies the place/occasion 'at the consistory' (meaning: by lingering at council meetings).
segments.paradiso-16-g104: L’oltracotata schiatta = 'the overweening/arrogant brood/lineage' (oltracotata is an adjective 'overweening'). Che introduces the relative clause. S’ (si) is the reflexive/impersonal pronoun and indraca is a poetic/rare verb-form here meaning 'becomes enraged/sets itself' (the provided lemma list lacks the relevant sense); dietro a chi fugge = 'after/against anyone who flees'. Note the relative clause modifies schiatta.
segments.paradiso-16-g105: E a chi mostra 'l dente o ver la borsa continues the relative description: e a chi = 'and against whoever', mostra is 3rd-sing. 'shows' or 'bears' (here 'bears/bares' his tooth — an idiom for threatening), ’l dente = 'the tooth' (figuratively: shows hostility), o ver la borsa = 'or toward the purse' (i.e. for money). The clause groups together targets of that arrogant brood.
segments.paradiso-16-g106: Com’ is the contracted form of come ('as' or 'just as'), agnel is the archaic form of agnello ('lamb'), and si placa is reflexive present tense of placare 'is pacified' / 'is calmed'. The phrase com’ agnel si placa is a simile inserted parenthetically: 'as a lamb is calmed.'
segments.paradiso-16-g107: Già venìa sù = già 'already' + venìa, an imperfect form ('was coming' / 'had begun to come') and sù 'up' indicating motion upward: 'was already coming up' (i.e. rising in influence). The imperfect gives a continuing or in-progress sense in the narrative past.
segments.paradiso-16-g108: Ma di picciola gente = 'but of small/lowly people'; ma 'but' introduces the contrast, di is partitive/of, picciola modifies gente creating the sense 'of humble origin' or 'from little stock', with gente as 'people' or 'stock'.
segments.paradiso-16-g109: Sì che = 'so that / therefore' introduces the result clause. Non piacque ad Ubertin Donato = 'it did not please Ubertin Donato' (piacque is passato remoto 'pleased' used impersonally). Che poï il suocero il fé lor parente is a subordinate clause introduced by che: poï 'afterwards', il suocero 'the father‑in‑law', il (accusative pronoun) 'him', fé (passato remoto of fare) 'made', lor 'to them', parente 'kinsman/relative' — 'that afterwards the father‑in‑law made him their kinsman.'
segments.paradiso-16-g110: Già era = 'already was'; ’l Caponsacco is a family name or nickname serving as the subject. Nel mercato 'in the marketplace' gives the location. Disceso (past participle of discendere) + giù da Fiesole indicates motion 'having come down from Fiesole' — the participle phrase describes Caponsacco's having descended to the market.
segments.paradiso-16-g111: E già era buon cittadino introduces another predicate: 'and already was a good citizen.' The inversion places the predicate before the proper names Giuda e Infangato (here functioning as personal/nicknames): 'and Judas and Infangato were already good citizens.'
segments.paradiso-16-g112: Io dirò is a first-person future 'I will say/tell' with Io emphatically stated. Cosa incredibile e vera is the object 'a thing incredible and true' — an emphatic promise of a surprising but truthful remark.
segments.paradiso-16-g113: Nel picciol cerchio = 'in/into the small circle' (picciol is poetic short form of piccolo). S’entrava is the impersonal/reflexive imperfect 'one entered / people used to enter' (si + imperfect), per porta 'through a gate'. Che si nomava da quei de la Pera is a relative clause: si nomava 'was called/named' (impersonal or passive reflexive) and da quei de la Pera means 'by those of the Pera' (i.e. named after the family/people called 'the Pera').
segments.paradiso-16-g129: Ma conveniesi a quella pietra scema che guarda 'l ponte, — Ma = 'but'. conveniesi is a literary, impersonal past (3rd-singular) meaning 'it was fitting' (from convenire); a + quella pietra scema marks the thing to which the fitness applies: 'befitted that/for that ...'. pietra scema is a noun + adjective phrase ('stone' + scema = 'diminished/dull/worn'); che guarda 'l ponte is a relative clause ('which watches/overlooks the bridge') modifying pietra.
segments.paradiso-16-g130: che Fiorenza fesse vittima ne la sua pace postrema. — che introduces the content/result clause dependent on conveniesi ('that ...'). Fiorenza is the subject. fesse is a literary past/subjunctive form (here best read as 'should be/made' or 'was made') of fare used in narrative/subordinate result; vittima is the direct object ('victim'). ne + la sua pace postrema = 'in/within her (its) last peace' (ne = 'in/during' here) giving the circumstance in which the victimhood occurred.
segments.paradiso-16-g131: Con queste genti, — Con = 'with'. queste genti = 'these peoples/people'; a short prepositional phrase establishing accompaniment/background for the seeing that follows.
segments.paradiso-16-g132: e con altre con esse, — e = 'and'. con altre = 'with others' (altre agrees with genti). con esse = 'with them' (repeating/including those others); this repetition is emphatic/pleonastic, a common poetic coordination meaning 'and with other people along with them'.
segments.paradiso-16-g133: vid' io Fiorenza in sì fatto riposo, — vid' (shortened vidi) is the archaic/poetic past 'I saw', here preceding the subject (verb–subject order for poetic effect). io is the explicit subject pronoun. in sì fatto riposo = 'in such/so great a repose' (sì = 'so', fatto = 'made/done' used adjectivally = 'such-made' = 'such'), indicating the state in which Florence was seen.
segments.paradiso-16-g134: che non avea cagione onde piangesse. — che introduces the result/explanatory clause ('so that ...'). non avea is the imperfect 'did not have' (avea = imperfect of avere). cagione = 'cause/reason'. onde = 'whereby/from which' (introducing the means/reason). piangesse is the imperfect subjunctive ('might/wept') describing a potential/result that did not obtain: 'no cause whereby she might weep.'
segments.paradiso-16-g135: Con queste genti vid'io glorïoso e giusto il popol suo, — Con queste genti (as above) sets accompaniment. vid'io = 'I saw' with the usual poetic verb-subject ordering. glorïoso e giusto are predicate adjectives describing il popol suo ('his/its people' — suo follows popol): 'glorious and just'. The adjective phrase follows the verb and directly modifies the object popol.
segments.paradiso-16-g136: tanto che 'l giglio non era ad asta mai posto a ritroso, — tanto che = 'so much that' introducing a result clause. 'l giglio = 'the lily' (the city's emblem). non era ... posto = imperfect passive/periphrastic 'was not placed' (era + past participle posto). ad asta = 'on a staff/pole' ('ad' = a + definite article elided), mai in negative contexts with non means 'ever'/'never' (non ... mai = never). a ritroso = 'backwards' indicating orientation.
segments.paradiso-16-g137: né per divisïon fatto vermiglio». — né = 'nor' (continuing the negative result). per divisïon = 'by/through division' (per marks cause/reason). fatto vermiglio = past-participle + adjective 'made vermilion/red' (fatto = 'made', vermiglio = 'vermilion/red'), describing that the lily had not been made red as a result of factional division.
segments.paradiso-16-g75: ‘E come’ introduces a simile (“And as…”). ‘’l volger’ is a nominalized form of volgere meaning ‘the turning/course’ (the moon’s motion). ‘del ciel de la luna’ is a double genitive/possessive phrase: literally “of the sky of the moon” = the moon’s course in the heavens. ‘cuopre e discuopre’ are present indicative, third-person singular verbs (poetic spellings of cuoprire/discuoprire) joined by e, meaning ‘covers and uncovers’. ‘i liti’ is the plural object (here “quarrels, disputes”), and ‘sanza posa’ (archaic sansa = senza) is an adverbial phrase “without rest/without pause”. The whole clause sets up the comparison continued in the next segment.
segments.paradiso-16-g76: ‘così’ = ‘thus/so’. Word order is inverted for emphasis: ‘di Fiorenza la Fortuna’ means ‘Fortune with respect to Florence’ (modern order would be la Fortuna di Fiorenza). ‘fa’ is present indicative 3rd singular of fare (“does/acts”), linking the simile to the subject Florence. The colon signals the poet will illustrate this claim.
segments.paradiso-16-g77: ‘per che’ here functions idiomatically as ‘therefore/wherefore’. ‘non dee parer’ uses the archaic modal dee (modern deve) plus the infinitive parer (‘appear/seem’): ‘ought not to seem’. ‘mirabil cosa’ = ‘a marvelous/strange thing’. ‘ciò ch’ io dirò’ = ‘that which I will say’ (ch’ = che). ‘de li alti Fiorentini’ = ‘about/of the lofty (high-born or eminent) Florentines’ (archaic article li). Finally ‘onde è la fama nel tempo nascosa’ links back to those Florentines: ‘from whom fame is in time hidden’ (onde = whence/from whom; nascosa = hidden), explaining why the forthcoming statements should not seem surprising.
segments.paradiso-16-g78: ‘Io vidi’ is passato remoto (I saw) repeated for emphasis: ‘I saw the Ughi and I saw the Catellini’. The articles li / i are archaic plural definite articles attached to family names: they mark the families/people named. The clause is a simple narrative listing.
segments.paradiso-16-g79: Single-name segment listing another family/name (Filippi). In context it is part of a catalog of Florentine families/people the narrator saw.
segments.paradiso-16-g80: Another name in the list (Greci). The form is nominative/plural and functions as a family name or group.
segments.paradiso-16-g81: ‘Ormanni e Alberichi’ continues the catalogue of family names joined by the conjunction e (‘and’).
segments.paradiso-16-g82: ‘già nel calare’ = ‘already in their decline/waning’. ‘già’ = already; ‘nel calare’ uses calare (literally ‘to descend’) figuratively as ‘decline/waning’ (of fortunes or importance).
segments.paradiso-16-g83: ‘illustri cittadini’ is a noun phrase describing those families: ‘illustrious citizens’. The adjective precedes the noun for emphasis/poetic rhythm.
segments.paradiso-16-g84: ‘e vidi così grandi come antichi’ resumes the narrator’s seeing: ‘and I saw (them) as great as they were ancient’. ‘così ... come’ is the correlative construction ‘as ... as’; ‘grandi’ and ‘antichi’ are plural adjectives agreeing with the implied people.
segments.paradiso-16-g85: ‘con quel de la Sannella’ literally ‘with that one of the Sannella’ — 'quel de' = 'the one from/of the (family)'. ‘con’ = with; de = archaic form of di. It identifies a particular person associated with the Sannella family.
segments.paradiso-16-g86: ‘quel de l’Arca’ is parallel to the previous phrase: ‘that one of the Arca’. The article is elided (l’) before Arca. The phrase points to an individual identified by a family epithet.
segments.paradiso-16-g87: A final run of names: ‘e Soldanieri e Ardinghi e Bostichi.’ Simple listing of family names, joined by repeated e (‘and’) for rhythm.
segments.paradiso-16-g88: ‘Sovra la porta’ = ‘above the gate’. Relative clause begins with ch’ (che): ‘al presente è carca’ = ‘at present is laden’ (è carca = is burdened). ‘di nova fellonia di tanto peso’ = ‘with new treachery of such weight’ (fellonia = treachery/felony). ‘che tosto fia iattura de la barca’ is a result clause introduced by che: ‘that soon will be a misfortune for the boat’ — fia is an archaic future (‘will be’), and ‘iattura de la barca’ uses the ship as a metaphor for the city.
segments.paradiso-16-g89: ‘erano i Ravignani’ uses the imperfect era/erano to state that the Ravignani family were present there ‘above the gate’. The plural imperfect marks ongoing/past presence rather than a single instantaneous event.
segments.paradiso-16-g90: ‘ond’ è disceso’ (onde contracted to ond’) = ‘whence/from which has descended’: a relative idea linking those Ravignani to a descendant. ‘il conte Guido’ is the noun phrase ‘Count Guido’. ‘e qualunque del nome de l’alto Bellincione ha poscia preso’ = ‘and whoever of the name of the exalted Bellincione has afterward taken (it)’: qualunque = ‘any/whichever’, ha poscia preso is a compound (ha preso) with poscia = afterwards; preso is past participle ‘taken/assumed’. The clause says these people are descended from or related to the named line.
segments.paradiso-16-g91: ‘Quel de la Pressa’ = ‘that one of the Pressa [family]’ (quel de = ‘the one from/of’). ‘sapeva’ is imperfect of sapere (‘knew’); here it means ‘knew how’ (knowledge/skill). ‘già’ = already. ‘come regger si vuole’ is an impersonal construction: regger (infinitive ‘to rule/govern’) + si vuole (‘is wanted/required’), meaning ‘how one must/ought to govern’. Word order is idiomatic and slightly archaic.
segments.paradiso-16-g92: ‘e avea’ = ‘and he had’ (avea = imperfect of avere). ‘Galigaio’ is the personal name. ‘dorata in casa sua già l’elsa e ’l pome’ literally ‘the hilt and the pommel already gilded in his house’: dorata is a past participle/adjective ‘gilded’ agreeing with the following nouns, in casa sua = ‘in his house’, già = already. The order places the adjective before the nouns for metrical/poetic reasons.
segments.paradiso-16-g114: Ciascun che introduces a relative clause ('each/everyone who ...'). de la is the archaic form of della ('of the'); bella insegna is the noun phrase 'fair banner' and porta here is the 3rd-person singular present of portare ('bears'). il cui ... e 'l cui ... uses the possessive cui twice ('whose ... and whose ...') to link nome and pregio to the gran barone; la festa di Tommaso riconforta is a subordinate clause (present tense) describing what the feast does to that name and praise.
segments.paradiso-16-g115: da esso ebbe milizia e privilegio; — da esso ('from him') shows the source; ebbe is passato remoto of avere used narratively ('received'/'had'); milizia e privilegio are the direct objects (military service/force and a special right).
segments.paradiso-16-g116: avvegna che is a concessive construction (archaic; 'avvegna che' = 'although'/'even though'). con popol si rauni uses the impersonal/reflexive si with raunare ('si rauni' = 'is/gets gathered' or 'gathers himself/is gathered'), and oggi colui che la fascia col fregio places the relative clause after colui ('he who ...'); col is the contraction of con + il and la fascia col fregio means 'girds/wears it (the sash) with the ornament.'
segments.paradiso-16-g117: Già 'already' sets the temporal frame; eran is the archaic imperfect 'erano' ('were'); Gualterotti e Importuni are plural proper names (clans/families) used as subjects.
segments.paradiso-16-g118: e ancor saria Borgo più quïeto, — ancor (ancora) = 'still/yet'; saria is the conditional form (archaic for sarebbe, 'would be'); Borgo is the subject and più quïeto is the comparative adjective 'more quiet' or 'quieter.'
segments.paradiso-16-g119: se introduces a conditional ('if'); di novi vicin is a partitive/description phrase where novi is archaic for 'new' (not the numeral) qualifying vicin ('neighbours'); fosser is the archaic subjunctive plural (fossero, 'were'); digiuni here means 'without' / 'unfamiliar with' / 'free of' (an older sense), so the clause says 'if it were free of the new neighbours.'
segments.paradiso-16-g120: La casa di che... — di che is an archaic relative 'from which'; nacque is passato remoto of nascere ('arose/was born' in the sense of 'gave rise to'); il vostro fleto = 'your lament' (vostro = possessive).
segments.paradiso-16-g121: per lo giusto disdegno che v'ha morti e puose fine al vostro viver lieto, — per introduces the cause ('because of'); lo giusto disdegno = 'the just disdain'; che introduces a relative clause. v' is the clitic vi ('to you'/'you'), and ha morti is an archaic/periphrastic perfect meaning 'has put you to death' or 'has slain you'; puose is passato remoto of porre ('put') used in 'puose fine' = 'put an end'; al vostro viver lieto = 'to your happy life.'
segments.paradiso-16-g122: era onorata, — era is the imperfect (was); onorata is a past participle used adjectivally/passively ('honoured').
segments.paradiso-16-g123: essa e suoi consorti: — essa ('it') refers back to la casa; e suoi consorti ('and its companions/associates') pairs the house with its allies/partners. Consorti here is plural, not limited to the modern 'spouse' sense.
segments.paradiso-16-g124: o Buondelmonte, — 'o' is the vocative interjection (O!, used to address directly) followed by the proper name Buondelmonte.
segments.paradiso-16-g125: quanto mal fuggisti le nozze süe per li altrui conforti! — quanto introduces an exclamation ('how'); mal fuggisti is a past action 'you fled badly/ill-advisedly' (fuggisti = you fled); le nozze süe = 'his/her nuptials' (süe = archaic possessive); per li altrui conforti = 'because of others' encouragements/assurances' (altrui = of others, conforti = encouragements/assistance).
segments.paradiso-16-g126: Molti = 'many'. sarebber is a contracted/poetic form of the conditional 'sarebbero' ('would be'). lieti = 'glad'/'happy'.
segments.paradiso-16-g127: Relative clause: che = 'who' referring back to Molti; son is the present 'are' (son for sono); tristi = 'sad'.
segments.paradiso-16-g128: A conditional clause introduced by se ('if'); Dio is the subject; t’ (ti) is the clitic object 'you'; avesse conceduto is the pluperfect subjunctive ('had granted') used in the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional; ad Ema names the place (Ema = the Arno/stream); la prima volta ch’a città venisti = 'the first time that you came to the city.'

sentences.paradiso-16-s5: My mind is filled by so many streams of gladness that it makes itself a joy, because it can endure without breaking.
sentences.paradiso-16-s6: Tell me then, my dear first-fruits: who were your forebears and which were the years that marked your childhood; tell me about the sheepfold of Saint John — how large it was then — and which people among it were worthy of the higher seats.
sentences.paradiso-16-s7: As a coal is rekindled into flame by the breathing of the winds, so I saw that light shine in answer to my blandishments; and as it became more beautiful to my eyes, so — with a sweeter and gentler voice, but not in this modern speech — it said to me: “From that day when ‘Ave’ was spoken at the childbirth in which my mother, who is now holy, was relieved of me, of which she had been burdened, this fire came back to be rekindled under her root at her Leo five hundred fifty and thirty times.”
sentences.paradiso-16-s8: My forebears and I were born in the place where the final sixth is found before those that run your annual course.
sentences.paradiso-16-s21: The Vaio column was already great: Sacchetti, Giuochi, Fifanti and Barucci, and Galli, and those who blush over the bushel.
sentences.paradiso-16-s22: The stock from which the Calfucci were born was already great, and they had already been chosen for the curule chairs Sizii and Arrigucci.
sentences.paradiso-16-s23: O what sorts of people I saw — those who are ruined by their pride!
sentences.paradiso-16-s24: And the balls of gold made Florence bloom in all her great deeds.
sentences.paradiso-16-s1: O small nobility of our blood: if you cause people here below — where our affection languishes — to boast of you, that will never be a marvelous thing to me; for there, where desire is not twisted, I say it in heaven, I took pride in it.
sentences.paradiso-16-s2: Indeed you are a cloak that soon shortens: so that, if days do not add themselves day by day, time goes around with the forces.
sentences.paradiso-16-s3: From the 'voi' which previously in Rome presented itself, in which his family persists less, my words began again; whereupon Beatrice, who was a little unreserved, laughing, seemed the one who coughed at the first mistake written about Ginevra.
sentences.paradiso-16-s4: I began: 'You are my father; you give me all boldness to speak; you raise me so that I am more than myself.'
sentences.paradiso-16-s9: Let it be enough to hear this of my elders: who they were and whence they came here; it is more fitting to be silent than to speak.
sentences.paradiso-16-s10: All those people who at that time were there, able to bear arms between Mars and St. John the Baptist, made up one-fifth of those who are now living.
sentences.paradiso-16-s11: But the citizenry, which is now mixed with those of Campi, Certaldo and Fegghine, appears pure in the last artisan.
sentences.paradiso-16-s12: Oh, how much better it would be for those peoples I speak of to be nearby — and to have your boundary at Galluzzo and at Trespiano — than to have them inside and endure the stench of the peasant of Aguglion, that one from Signa, who already has a sharp eye for bargaining!
sentences.paradiso-16-s13: If the people in the world who stray most had not been a stepmother to Caesar, but, like a mother, kind to her own son, then such a deed — if it were Florentine — would change hands and be traded; it would have turned toward the Simifonti, where their ancestor went on the hunt; Montemurlo would still belong to the Conti; the Cerchi would be in the Piovier d'Acone, and perhaps the Buondelmonti in the Val di Greve.
sentences.paradiso-16-s14: The confusion of persons has always been the origin of the city's evil, just as your food that is put in (your city); and a blind bull falls more quickly than a blind lamb; and many times one (man) cuts more and better than the five swords.
sentences.paradiso-16-s15: If you look back at Luni and Orbisaglia as they have gone, and how Chiusi and Sinigaglia go off after them, to hear how lineages fall apart will not seem to you a new or startling thing, since cities have their limits.
sentences.paradiso-16-s16: All your things have their death, just as you do; but in some (cases) it hides itself and endures a long time, and lives are short.
sentences.paradiso-16-s25: Thus did the fathers of those who, whenever your church is vacant, grow fat while sitting in consistory.
sentences.paradiso-16-s26: The overweening stock that sets itself against anyone who flees, and against anyone who shows his teeth or his purse — as a lamb is pacified — was already coming up, but of humble (or little) people; so that it did not please Ubertin Donato that afterwards the father‑in‑law made him their kinsman.
sentences.paradiso-16-s27: By then Caponsacco was already in the market, come down from Fiesole, and Judas and Infangato were already good citizens.
sentences.paradiso-16-s28: I will say something incredible and true: into the small circle one entered through a gate that was called after those of the Pera.
sentences.paradiso-16-s33: But it was fitting for that dull stone which watches the bridge that Florence should be made a victim in her last peace.
sentences.paradiso-16-s34: With these peoples, and with others together with them, I saw Florence in so great a repose that she had no reason whereby she might weep.
sentences.paradiso-16-s35: With these peoples I saw her people glorious and just, so that the lily was never placed on a staff backwards, nor made vermilion by division.
sentences.paradiso-16-s17: And as the moon's revolution in the sky covers and uncovers quarrels without pause, so does Fortune act with regard to Florence; therefore it should not seem a marvelous thing what I shall say of the lofty Florentines, from whom Fame in time is hidden.
sentences.paradiso-16-s18: I saw the Ughi and the Catellini, Filippi, Greci, Ormanni and Alberichi — already in decline, illustrious citizens; and I saw others as great as they were ancient, with the one of the Sannella, the one of the Arca, and Soldanieri, Ardinghi and Bostichi.
sentences.paradiso-16-s19: Above the gate that at present is laden with a new treachery so heavy that soon it will be a calamity for the ship, there were the Ravignani, whence descended Count Guido, and whoever of the name of the high Bellincione has afterwards taken it.
sentences.paradiso-16-s20: The one of the Pressa already knew how one should govern, and Galigaio already had in his house the hilt and the pommel gilded.
sentences.paradiso-16-s29: Everyone who bears the fair banner of the great baron, whose name and whose honour the feast of Thomas renews, received military service and privilege from him; although today he who girds the sash with its ornament is gathered with the people.
sentences.paradiso-16-s30: The Gualterotti and the Importuni were already there; and Borgo would be even quieter still, if it were free of the new neighbors.
sentences.paradiso-16-s31: The house from which your lament arose, by the just disdain that has slain you and put an end to your happy life, was honoured — it and its companions: O Buondelmonte, how ill-advisedly you fled his nuptials at the urging of others!
sentences.paradiso-16-s32: Many would be glad who are sad, if God had granted you to the Ema the first time you came to the city.
